ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised pork with Chinese cabbage; mapped to broader pork dish class FOODON_03315671.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for braised pork preparation; no composite dish code available for this specific combination.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific composite code for braised pork with Chinese cabbage; A0FJP (cooked pork) used as the primary ingredient anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; not clinically coded as a distinct food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised pork with Chinese cabbage is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one medium bowl containing a mix of braised pork pieces and softened napa cabbage in savoury braising liquid.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Pork with Chinese Cabbage — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Braised Pork with Chinese Cabbage (cr_id F06100010). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical source at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork belly braised entry (NDB 10006) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate braised pork and non-starchy vegetable dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised pork with Chinese cabbage. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the dish's dominant composition: high protein (9.4 g/100g), moderate fat (7.3 g/100g), and very low carbohydrate (1.04 g/100g). The University of Sydney GI Database classifies high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat-and-vegetable dishes in the GI range 30–40. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 1.04 × 2) / 100 = 0.73, rounded to 0.7. This is negligible and consistent with a very low GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 196 mg/100g; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 392 mg sodium, representing ~17% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less per meal and request low-sodium soy sauce and reduced oyster sauce to keep per-serving sodium below 300 mg; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as additional napa cabbage can help offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.04 g/100g) and negligible sugar (0.24 g/100g) result in a glycemic load of less than 1 per serving, producing minimal postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a protein-rich, low-glycaemic option; however, portion control of the pork component is advised to manage total caloric and saturated fat intake, and soy-based sauces should be confirmed for added sugar content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork belly contributes saturated fat (estimated ~2.65 g/100g) and the dish contains 196 mg sodium/100g; both are relevant cardiovascular risk factors at higher portion sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to a 150 g portion, choose leaner pork cuts such as pork loin over pork belly to reduce saturated fat, use low-sodium soy sauce, and increase the proportion of Chinese cabbage to improve the fibre-to-fat ratio of the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is a red meat containing arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which can amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways relevant to joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting pork with chicken breast or firm tofu to reduce arachidonic acid load, and increase the proportion of Chinese cabbage which provides vitamin C and anti-inflammatory phytonutrients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pork with Chinese Cabbage (白菜炖猪肉) is a classic Singaporean Chinese home-style dish in which pork — typically pork belly or shoulder — is slow-braised together with napa cabbage (Chinese cabbage) in a savoury sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, ginger, garlic, and sesame oil. The extended braising process renders the pork tender and succulent while the cabbage absorbs the rich umami braising liquid, becoming soft and flavourful. The dish has a mild, savoury-sweet flavour profile with a silky, moist texture. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 108 kcal, 9.4 g protein, 7.3 g fat, and only 1.04 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a negligible glycemic load. Sodium is 196 mg/100g, which is moderate and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate and sugar content. It is not suitable for strict halal diets as it contains pork. Clinically, the saturated fat from pork belly and the sodium from soy-based sauces are the primary nutritional cautions for cardiovascular and hypertensive patients. The dish is commonly served as part of a multi-dish Chinese family meal alongside steamed rice and other vegetable dishes.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact dish match (cr_id F06100010). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central braised pork belly data as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions. GI is an analogous estimate documented with approved source basis. Overall confidence is high given the primary source is canonical and exact.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.65 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for braised pork belly (NDB 10006 / FDC ID 167903) as the HPB Foodo database does not report saturated fat breakdown for this dish; this is an adapted value and should be verified if fatty acid profiling is required. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this dish, and no sufficiently close composite dish entry was found in USDA FoodData Central to support reliable estimation; detailed fatty acid and micronutrient data for this traditional Singaporean Chinese braised preparation are not available in approved sources as of 2026. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 4. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. 5. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input is retained as the dish has 1.04 g carbohydrate/100g, which is consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds. 6. Calcium (20.0 mg/100g) and iron (0.97 mg/100g) are reported directly from HPB Foodo source.
